text: you wake up when your body is ready.
No alarm, no notification, no meeting in 20 minutes.
The sun has barely touched the horizon and your body simply knows it is always
known.
For roughly 300,000 years, this was every single morning for your ancestors.
No schedule waiting, no boss, no inbox, just the world your people
and the slow unfolding of a day that looked almost nothing like yours.
So what did they actually do with all that time?
The answer will probably make you uncomfortable.
Anthropologist Marshall Salons famously called Hunter Gatherers the original affluent society, not
because they had much, but because they wanted little and the gap between what they needed and what the world provided was
surprisingly small.
His 1966 analysis of the Kung San people of the Kalahari Desert found that adults spent
an average of just 15 to 20 hours per week on subsistence activities, hunting, gathering,
preparing food, everything needed to survive.
15 to 20 hours per week.
You probably spend more than that commuting.
The rest of the time was not idle, but it was not what you would call productive either.
It was something older than productivity, something your nervous system still craves
and almost never gets.
Let's reconstruct a morning.
You rise with four or five others in your band.
Maybe a dozen people total are camp nearby.
The fire from the night before still breathes with a few orange coals.
Someone feeds it.
Someone else begins checking the small clay pots near the edge of the embers.
There is no rush.
There is no particular order to any of this.
Children are awake, but no one is managing them.
They wander toward the fire, toward the trees, toward each other.
Anthropologist Peter Gray, who has spent decades studying play in Hunter Gatherer societies, found that children
in these groups had almost unlimited free play from the time they could walk.
No structured learning, no lessons, just relentless self-directed exploration, and somehow
by adolescence they had absorbed everything they needed to know, tracking plant identification, fire
making, social navigation, all of it learned sideways through watching and doing and failing in
low-stakes settings.
You were not educated, you were immersed.
By mid-morning the day's real activity begins.
It depends on the season.
It depends on what the land is offering.
If it is a hunting day, two or three of the men and sometimes women depending on the group will head out.
Not all of them.
Never all of them.
The Hads of Tanzania, one of the last true Hunter Gatherer societies still living today and studied extensively
by anthropologist Frank Marlow, typically hunt in small groups of two to four.
The hunt might last three hours.
It might last seven.
It might produce nothing at all.
Failure is not catastrophic.
It is Tuesday.
Meanwhile, back at camp, the gathering continues.
Women and older men move through a familiar patch of landscape.
A patch they know the way you know your own kitchen.
They know which plants are edible now and which will be ready in three weeks.
They know where the roots run deepest after rain.
They know the animal trails and the bird calls and what it means when the bees move north.
This knowledge is not written anywhere.
It lives in bodies and in stories.
Researcher Polly Weasner spent 40 years studying the juice-lash-honcy of Southern Africa, specifically
their conversations.
She found something remarkable.
During the day, talk was practical, where to find food.
What someone did wrong?
Immediate concrete local concerns.
But at night around the fire something shifted.
Nighttime talk was almost entirely stories.
Stories about distant relatives, about past hunts, about spirits and journeys and people long dead.
Weasner called this fireside talk the original social media.
The camp fire was where the human imagination expanded, where culture was transmitted,
where identity was maintained across generations that would never meet face to
face.
You are around the fire by mid afternoon.
The successful gatherers have returned with mongongo nuts, tubers, wild berries.
Someone who knows the plants has sorted what is edible and what is not.
Food is shared not because of charity but because of a deeply embedded social technology called demand sharing.
The most hunter-gatherer society's hoarding is not just frowned upon.
It is dangerous.
It marks you as someone who has placed themselves outside the group.
And outside the group, historically, you die.
There is no refrigerator.
There is no storage.
What is found today is eaten today or shared until it is gone.
The result is that no one goes hungry when anyone has food.
The result is also that accumulation is structurally impossible.
You cannot build wealth in a society where wealth is immediately redistributed.
This is not a political statement.
It is an anthropological one.
The economist James Sussman, who spent years living with the Jew-slash-Honcy, described this as a fiercely-galitarianism,
not peaceful, not passive but actively enforced.
Anyone who started to act superior, who bragged, who claimed more than their share, was socially dismantled,
through teasing, through gossip, through what anthropologists call leveling mechanisms, the nail that
stuck up got hammered down every time.
Your ancestors did not dream of rising above.
They dreamed of staying in.
As the sun drops lower, the day slows.
Children have been running, climbing, inventing games with sticks and stones for hours.
Adult snap.
Rest is not laziness here.
It is physiology.
Your body without electric light or caffeine follows something called by phasic sleep, a natural
rhythm of two-sleep periods separated by hours of quiet wakefulness.
The nap is not a luxury.
It is what your body was built for in a world before someone decided that sixteen
hours of continuous productivity was the mark of a serious person.
Studies of traditional societies that maintain natural light cycles research conducted by
anthropologist Carol Werthman among others consistently show sleep patterns radically
different from modern Western norms, not just the nap.
The night sleep itself is more fragmented, more communal, punctuated by soft conversation and
the tending of fires.
No one sleeps alone if they can help it.
The silence of sleeping in a sealed room alone in total darkness would
have been terrifying to your ancestors.
It would have meant something had gone very wrong.
The evening comes, the fire is rebuilt, the day's stories begin.
Someone narrates the hunt with dramatic gestures.
Someone else imitates the sound of a bird to settle a dispute about identification.
Children who should theoretically be exhausted are still awake, listening, contributing welcomed into the
adult conversation without being shushed.
The hadza have no bedtime.
Sleep arrives when the body asks for it, which under natural conditions is usually within an hour
or two of full dark.
And then the fire burns low and then silence.
Now think about your day yesterday.
When did you last let your body wake without an alarm?
When did you last spend three hours in unstructured time with people you love without a
screen nearby without anything to accomplish?
When did you last eat a meal that someone in your immediate circle had found or made from scratch
shared with everyone present without anyone counting the cost?
The archaeologist Stephen Mithin, studying the cognitive evolution of early humans, argued
that the richness of prehistoric social life was not a primitive version of civilization.
It was a different kind of civilization.
One optimized not for output but for belonging, not for accumulation, but for sufficiency,
not for the future but for the felt quality of the present moment.
You still carry that optimization in your body.
It is why silence feels wrong and voices feel right.
It is why you crave being watched and known.
It is why your brain releases dopamine during storytelling and oxytocin during sustained
eye contact and serotonin when you feel like you matter to the people around you.
You were not built for the office, you were built for the fire.
The alarm goes off, you reach for your phone before your eyes fully open.
Your ancestors would not have recognized any of this, but somewhere underneath
the schedule and the inbox and the fluorescent light, your nervous system still knows
exactly what a real morning feels like.
It is waiting for you to remember.
